sentences.text: Spell them.
I was-- I'm Gizella Kleinman.
K-L-E-I-N-M-A-N G-E-L-L-A-L-A.
It's two Ls, actually.
G-E-Z-E-L-L-A.
Birth.
My maiden name?
Steinberg.
S-T-E-N-B-E-R.
Steinberg.
Yes, I have-- I have two names.
Yiddish.
Gitl, Mindl.
I'm born in 1925, 5th of May.
Mukacevo.
Czechoslovakia.
M-U-K-A-N-C-H.
Well, uh, my father, my father was a book binder and a printer.
He did printing and book binding.
And my mother was a-- a housewife.
Didn't work.
Uh-- Herman and Fani.
Well, um, he had a good business.
My father also worked for, uh, um, Hungary, Budapest.
He also worked for Poland.
[PAUSE] We had a good life.
Made a good living.
Well, um, he was, um, he was binding new things.
Everything was new.
He didn't work with old books or something like that.
And most of them was Jewish books.
And he binded with leather, and he printed with gold things, with gold.
And his work was beautiful.
Sometime I see, uh, books, and I'm looking if it's not from I'm father, but it's not, because it's not leather.
Um-- he also worked with a few people we had who was working with us.
And, uh-- [PAUSE]
Uh-- [PAUSE] --it's a nice life.
How large was his business?
How many people worked with him?
About five.
It wasn't very, very big, but he imported also.
You know, when he was working, he imported to Poland, to Budapest.
He used to make those little books.
It looked like a purse.
It's for the girls who got married.
I remember it cost 100, uh, what do you call it?
Koruna?
But Czechoslovakian money, it was 100.
It was a lot of money at that time.
Well, uh, it was one person also he had that kind of a business, but he wasn't a book binder.
He wasn't.
He was working also for him.
He was selling those books.
And, uh, he was just-- he had a big place.
And what my father sold to him, he sold it to the people.
My father didn't sell that somebody's coming in, and he wants a book.
It wasn't that way.
He made-- it's hard to say in English, but, uh, it's called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like, let's say 20 books in one thing, you know.
And that's how he sold his things what he was making.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My mother was a wonderful person.
She liked to make-- she is-- you know, we had seven kids in the house.
And it wasn't easy, but we had a little help in the house.
We had like a maid in the house.
It's not like a maid, it was a maid.
Um-- My mother was most with the children.
She was a very good mother.
That was her whole life, I think.
And, uh, she also helped out other people.
Well, um, like [INAUDIBLE] was a lot of people going begging, you know.
They didn't have food and used to come Monday and Thursday, used to come begging.
So they knew that they're coming in the house, there is a plate of soup, a piece of bread, and some money.
It was very important for my mother to do, and she did it twice a week.
Most the same people coming down from the small villages.
There was a lot of poor people there.
It's not like here where they have, you know, they help out.
They have welfare, they have the-- we didn't have that.
Jewish people, but poor people.
Um--
She used to sew for us little clothes, beautiful things she used to make for us.
Sometimes for the neighbor also.
Not for money, just for friendship.
Uh-- in Europe, they didn't-- you didn't have like clubs or things like that that you belonged there or something like that.
Eh-- I don't know.
I think there was a lot of work also at home for, uh-- with seven kids.
[SIGHS] His name, uh, my older brother, was [?
Yeshua ?] Steinberg.
He was a yeshiva bocher.
And he, uh, he was learning, uh, most of time until about 20 years he was in the yeshiva.
He wasn't at the home.
He was until, uh, his bar mitzvah he was home, but then they send them away to another country called, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a big yeshiva there.
And, uh, after that-- oh, he also went in a non-Jewish school too, not just an yeshiva alone, you know?
And, uh, after he-- he married young.
He was about 22.
Got married, had a child.
Little girl.
His wife was [INAUDIBLE].
And, uh, she came from a very, very orthodox home.
Her father was a shochet.
Please tell us what a shochet is.
He, uh, he kills chickens.
[LAUGHS] Chickens and cows and, you know, animals.
But for kosher.
That's a shochet.
And, um, yeah, he opened up after, um-- he opened up a store Um-- and, uh, very happy and made a good living.
He had a store from, um, material.
Well, he was, um-- four years older.
Four years older.
Well, here's-- my mother had one after another.
And, um-- That was about it.
They wouldn't weren't long married till they're working.
My brother, who is now not alive anymore.
Menachem Mendel.
Well, Menachem Mendel, he was also little while a yeshiva bocher, and, uh-- and he went there with my father on business.
And, uh-- when a Hungarian came in, he didn't like him.
He didn't like the Hungarian.
And also somehow, I don't know what happened.
Somebody hit him.
And he decided not to live there.
And he went with a few friends.
He went to Russia.
It was in 1938.
And, uh, they thought that he's a spy.
And they took him to, uh-- Kapa-- no.
I told you where they put him.
I forgot.
Um-- Sibir.
And he was there till, uh, he was liberated.
And then when we came here, he came.
He decided that we're going to go too.
He came to Germany.
He find me.
I didn't see him for years.
Yeah.
My older sister, Brena [PAUSE] Well, she also [INAUDIBLE] went to school and, uh, wonderful girl.
I miss her.
And, uh, she went through high school.
I-- and then she decided to go in-- uh, she also went in my hu-- my father's business but that was later.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
That was later on, uh.
[INAUDIBLE] the next, and then I came.
[PAUSE] So, uh-- there was also the Hungarian came in, so we had a hard time also.
She went also through Czechoslovakian school.
And, um, [PAUSE] when Hungarian came in, we had to start all over.
It was very hard.
And it was a terrible life.
And that you want later.
You want it later?
Let's finish talking about your siblings right now.
Oh.
After my sister who died, Helen.
Well, she also-- she went to school and-- in Czechoslovakian school.
And, uh, that was it, school.
[PAUSE] And, uh-- but she didn't belong nowhere like, you know, uh, it was nothing.
That was the life, like--
My brother, Yittel.
That's the-- after Helen.
Well, he went-- he went to, like a parochial school.
I mean, he went to Jewish school in-- like a Yeshiva, and also he went to English school.
And, um, he was young.
[PAUSE] Uh-- and then I had my younger sister, Malka.
But she was very young too.
And she also in school.
Um-- I think, um-- he was after bar mitzvah, I think, just after bar mitzvah, about 13.
And the youngest was, um, uh-- she was 9 or 10.
I don't remember exactly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We had a large home, a very big home, a matter of fact.
That you don't want to I should say that after the war I came home, I find some Russians made a hospital.
That's how big it was.
It was a house for us.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We had a big back and a big front and the house for us, just nobody else was living there.
It was like separate, you know.
Yeah.
Well, the-- well, the girls had-- two girls and two-- I had with one older sister a room.
And, um-- uh-- my younger sister and the youngest sister, the small one, they were sleeping together.
And it's the same thing was with the boys.
But the boys, uh, they weren't really-- well, yeah, when they were small.
Favorite?
Between my-- my famil-- sister?
Oh.
Well, we had a big dining room which like-- you know, homework to make and things like that.
I liked that.
And, um--
Because it was huge, and you could move around, and, uh-- and I could have friends over.
And, uh, with sitting, making homework, and things like that.
And after that, we, you know, we had a big family.
So we were eating there.
[LAUGHS] We had meals there.
And--
[SIGHS] Very good one.
My father made a good living.
Had-- we never ate alone.
We always had people over.
My mother, like for Friday, she knew that she has to make for 10 people more.
If my father was going to go to shul, he was going to bring some people over, which was always had people.
And then we had a-- a boy who was learning also and used to come for a meal.
And, uh, we had, for Shabbat we had-- matter of fact, I'll find them in a book there.
A small little Jewish man with a cane.
He used to eat every Shabbat with us.
And, uh, we had like-- we were never alone, like always people over.
And, uh, very friendly home.
Friends.
I could bring anybody up.
Anybody had a meal.
It was a good life.
Oh, that was a big preparation.
[LAUGHS] My mother started really to bake Thursday.
And Friday she usually made-- she baked challahs Friday.
The cakes she made on Thursday already.
And, um-- there was a lot of food prepared, um, because we knew that we're going to have people over.
And they-- they were very poor people who had the meal with us.
And my father used to put them on the top of the-- the table where he used to sit.
He used to put them there.
Sometimes he brought over people who were awful.
You know.
He brought one man.
He was very, very religious.
He knew a lot, you know.
In Jewish, he was like a big rabbi, but he wasn't all there.
So when he made kiddush, he used to say, pick pick.
When I'm crap.
And we-- uh, we weren't allowed to laugh, because that was a sin.
[LAUGHS] That was terrible.
We used to-- we must be quiet.
But like running out and laughing a little bit.
He used to-- all kind of people he used to bring over.
But there were poor people.
That man who, uh, was that little man on the Shabbat he used to come.
He wasn't all there either.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, yes.
I remember his name.
And I find him from Munkacs.
There is somebody who had that book, and I looked through, and I said, oh, who is here.
He used to go around and used to beg, goes around begging, like people, you know.
So everybody used to tell what kind of a Shabbat he has pass, how good it was.
How many [INAUDIBLE] he ate.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Very orthodox.
My mother was wearing a wig.
My father was wearing a shtreimel.
You know what shtreimel means?
It's like a hat, and-- and-- and it's a round-- with four.
You never said?
[PAUSE] My mother came up in a very orthodox home and a very smart-- I mean, very smart people.
One was a judge, his-- her brother.
The other one was a shochet-- you know, the--
As matter of fact, there was a doctor also in the family.
We just find out now the kids from that doctor.
He's dead.
Well, he was in, ah, Budapest.
He also was yeshiva bocher, but then he decided to go into medicine.
And he went to Budapest and became a big doctor.
He was a surgeon.
Um-- what do you call it, a head surgeon, maybe?
And how were they related to you?
It's, uh, my mother-- uh, my mother's, um, uncle.
That's my grandfather's brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, uh, they were very orthodox, uh, family.
Well, it was very strict.
We, uh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] have to pray, you know, the girls.
Well, I didn't go to a parochial school, but we had somebody who was coming in, you know, and teach.
That's because they didn't really have a girl's-- they didn't have a orthodox school.
They didn't have, so, uh-- but, uh-- I still remain-- I'm not that orthodox like my mother, but my home, it's-- my children are orthodox.
Peggy, children, and, uh-- I remain like that because I come up from home with it.
And, uh-- [PAUSE] Well, you see-- you say, well, how could you live so much through and what happened this and this.
But when it comes to life, you go back what you saw at home.
I mean, not everybody, but I did.
My younger sister-- my sister who had died, was very, very orthodox.
She lived in Bnei Brak.
She left $1 million for the yeshivas.
She built-- she built a school for 300 children, a yeshiva.
She built-- there is the rabbi in Bnei Brak.
She built for him a place where he has-- he-- the people when they're coming, they're coming in that place, and the name is there.
She built also for people who can't afford, they want to make a wedding and bar mitzvahs and things like that.
She built that too, but she never had children.
That was [INAUDIBLE].
My favorite holiday was Pesach.
[INAUDIBLE] I got some nice things always.
[LAUGHS] New things.
Yeah.
You had a dress.
You had shoes.
You had everything new for every [INAUDIBLE] activity.
[INAUDIBLE] but Pesach was, you know, somehow, not for my mother.
My mother wasn't too good, because she had to work hard.
But for the children, we loved it.
We had a very nice-- nice.
Uh--
